name: Fiji

colors.symbolism: the Pacific Ocean surrounding the islands and the clear sky
the field of the shield and the saltire and cross of the Union Flag canton
the cross of St George on the shield, the cross of St George and saltire of St Patrick within the Union Flag canton

description: A light sky-blue field with the Union Flag of the United Kingdom in the upper-hoist canton (one quarter of the flag's area) and the shield from the Fijian coat of arms — a red cross of St George dividing a white field, with a British lion holding a cocoa pod in chief and emblems of sugar cane, coconut palm, dove of peace and bananas in the four quarters — centred in the fly half.
history: Adopted on 10 October 1970, the day Fiji gained independence from the United Kingdom. The flag is essentially a defaced light-blue ensign retaining the Union Flag canton and the colonial-era shield (granted by royal warrant in 1908), with the dark blue of the Blue Ensign replaced by a lighter sky-blue chosen to represent the Pacific. Despite Fiji becoming a republic in 1987 and ceasing to be a Commonwealth realm, the British symbols have been retained; proposals to redesign the flag were considered in 2015–2016 but ultimately abandoned.

features.symbolism: a defaced British Light Blue Ensign — light sky-blue chosen to represent the Pacific Ocean which surrounds the islands and on which the people depend
Fiji's history as a British colony and continuing membership of the Commonwealth of Nations

features.role: shield from the Fijian coat of arms
features.symbolism: the shield of the national arms granted in 1908: a red cross of St George on a white field divides the shield into quarters; the chief bears a yellow British lion holding a cocoa pod, and the four quarters depict sugar cane, a coconut palm, a dove of peace and a bunch of bananas — Fiji's principal agricultural products and the nation's commitment to peace
